results.proposal_summary: The European Union, through its EU-Philippine Green Economy Partnership and managed by Expertise France in partnership with the Department of Trade and Industry (DTI), is launching the 'Green Up to Scale Up: MSME Transformation Grant' in the Philippines. With a total grant pool of EUR 1.8 million (PHP 125 million), the call aims to accelerate the circular and green transition of Filipino Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) in priority industries including agriculture and fisheries, tourism and hospitality, food, and textile. Eligible applicants are network-rooted actors (MSME business support networks and related private sector players) based in the Philippines. The grant will fund the establishment and implementation of business support programmes enabling MSME circular transformation. The application deadline has been extended to 30 April 2026. No co-financing requirement is explicitly stated. The ILO, as an international organization, is not an eligible applicant for this call.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the grant focuses on sustainable enterprises and green economic transition (themes tangentially related to ILO's just transition and sustainable enterprises mandate), it is primarily a business support/circular economy grant for Filipino MSMEs and does not directly address core ILO mandates such as labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or social dialogue.

results.deadline_text: Deadline: 11 May, 2026, 18:00 (Kyiv time)

results.proposal_summary: The European Union, through Internews International's FAIR Media Ukraine project, invites Ukrainian media outlets from eleven frontline regions (Donetsk, Luhansk, Kharkiv, Sumy, Chernihiv, Zaporizhzhia, Kherson, Mykolaiv, Odesa, Dnipropetrovsk, and Kyiv) to apply for grants averaging EUR 15,000 each for projects lasting up to eight months. The competition aims to support regional and local media operating under wartime conditions to continue delivering trusted, relevant information and strengthen their medium-term resilience. Eligible applicants must be media outlets registered in Ukraine that serve frontline audiences, demonstrate editorial independence, maintain an operational presence in their covered regions, and must not have previously received a FAIR Media Ukraine grant. The deadline for submission is 11 May 2026 at 18:00 Kyiv time. No co-financing is required. The ILO is not eligible as applicants are restricted to Ukrainian-registered media outlets, and the grant size is below the USD 50,000 threshold.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This grant focuses on media sustainability and wartime information provision in Ukraine's frontline regions, which does not align with the ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The European Union, through a joint project of UNDP and the Anti-Corruption Agency of the Republic of Uzbekistan titled 'Strengthening the National Anti-Corruption Ecosystem in Uzbekistan', announces a small grants competition 'Civil Initiatives Against Corruption: Participation, Transparency and Solutions' targeting civil society organisations across all regions of Uzbekistan. The competition supports anti-corruption initiatives in four priority areas: civil society participation in law-making, digital transparency tools, women's integrity and economic empowerment, and local-level anti-corruption. The total competition budget is USD 30,000 and the maximum grant per project is USD 6,000 (paid in national currency). Implementation period is up to 6 months. Eligible applicants are exclusively national non-governmental non-profit organisations (including women's and youth organisations), and academic/research institutions registered and operating in Uzbekistan whose statutory objectives align with the competition theme. Commercial organisations, government bodies, organisations under liquidation, and individuals are not eligible. Applications must be submitted in Uzbek or Russian to anti-corruption.contest@undp.org by 00:00 Tashkent time on 4 May 2026. Attendance at a mandatory online information session (17 or 24 April 2026) is required prior to application.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the competition touches on transparency, civic engagement, and women's economic empowerment which partially relate to ILO themes (equality, decent work), its core focus is anti-corruption in general governance contexts rather than labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or other core ILO mandate areas.

Eligible applicants are civil society organisations and non-state actors operating on a non-profit basis (including NGOs, diaspora organisations, social enterprises, universities, research centres) registered in an AU or EU Member State and established for at least 2 years; international organisations such as the ILO are not eligible. results.title: Adaptation Benefit Mechanism (ABM) - African Development Bank
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies
eligible_activities: Solar powered irrigation pumps

results.proposal_summary: The Adaptation Benefit Mechanism (ABM) is an initiative of the African Development Bank (AfDB) designed to mobilize public and private sector finance for climate change adaptation in Africa through a results-based finance model. The mechanism certifies social, economic, and environmental adaptation benefits and promotes these certificates to potential investors or lenders. Piloted from 2019 to 2023 across approximately 10-12 demonstration projects in Africa (now concluded), ABM covers a broad scope including resilient agriculture, coastal protection, clean energy, and weather information systems. This page is an informational overview of the mechanism and does not constitute an open Call for Proposals with a defined grant amount, eligible applicant categories, or submission deadline. The pilot phase has concluded and a permanent secretariat is being established by 2027.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The ABM focuses on climate change adaptation finance in Africa with some references to job creation for vulnerable groups and women's economic empowerment, which tangentially aligns with ILO's just transitions and decent work mandates, but the mechanism is primarily an environmental finance tool with no direct focus on labour standards, employment policy, or social protection.

results.proposal_summary: The ADF Climate Action Window is a dedicated financing facility established by the African Development Fund (ADF) under its 16th replenishment cycle to address the approximately $60 billion annual climate financing gap faced by low-income African countries. The Window allocates resources across three sub-windows: 75% for adaptation, 15% for mitigation, and 10% for technical assistance, covering six thematic sectors including agriculture and food security, water security, climate information and early warning, green transport and infrastructure, green energy and energy efficiency, and green finance. Eligible recipients are ADF-member low-income African countries (sub-Saharan Africa and other ADF-eligible states). The facility offers a large grant component to minimize debt distress risks. No specific submission deadline or maximum grant size is stated in the available text. As the programme targets sovereign governments rather than international organisations, the ILO as a UN agency would not qualify as an eligible applicant.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the ADF Climate Action Window touches on just transition themes (green energy, green infrastructure, adaptation), it is primarily a sovereign climate finance facility for African governments and does not align with the ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection, or social dialogue.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Adaptation Acceleration Program (AAAP) is a joint initiative of the African Development Bank (AfDB) and the Global Center on Adaptation (GCA), aiming to mobilize $25 billion over five years to accelerate climate adaptation across Africa through four pillars: climate-smart digital agriculture (targeting 30 million farmers in 26 countries), infrastructure resilience, youth empowerment for green jobs (1 million youth), and innovative adaptation finance. The program operates through an AAAP Upstream Financing Facility at GCA and the ADF Climate Action Window providing up to $13 billion for low-income African countries. This page is an informational program description rather than a Call for Proposals; no submission deadline, eligible applicant categories, or specific grant size for external applicants are defined. The ILO contributed the 'Jobs' chapter to the associated State and Trends in Adaptation 2021 report, indicating potential thematic alignment.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Circular Economy Facility (ACEF) is a trust fund established by the African Development Bank (AfDB) in 2022, with support from the Government of Finland, the Nordic Development Fund, and The Coca-Cola Foundation. It aims to mainstream the circular economy as an inclusive and green growth strategy across African countries, operating through three pillars: enabling environment for circularity, circular business development, and advocacy and knowledge sharing. ACEF provides grants and technical assistance to projects across Africa. However, this page is an informational overview of the facility and does not constitute an open Call for Proposals. theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Disaster Risk Financing Programme (ADRiFi), initiated by the African Development Bank (AfDB) in 2018, aims to enhance the resilience of African countries to climate shocks by improving disaster risk management and financing. The programme's Multi-Donor Trust Fund (MDTF), established in 2021 and funded by the United Kingdom, Switzerland, Canada, Norway, the Netherlands, and the United States (~USD 96.7 million total), provides premium subsidies and capacity development support to AfDB Regional Member Countries (sovereign governments) across Africa. It is implemented in partnership with the African Risk Capacity (ARC) Group. All AfDB Regional Member Countries are eligible upon formal request to the Bank and meeting requirements for participation in the ARC regional risk pool. This is not an open Call for Proposals for external organizations; there is no defined application deadline or per-organization grant size. The ILO is not an eligible applicant under this programme framework.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While ADRiFi touches on livelihoods and food security for vulnerable rural workers (including women in agriculture), its core focus is on climate disaster risk financing and insurance for sovereign governments, which falls outside the ILO's primary mandate of labour standards, employment, and social protection.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Fertilizer Financing Mechanism (AFFM) is a special fund administered by the African Development Bank Group, established by the African Union in 2006 to address low fertilizer use and stagnant agricultural productivity in Africa. The mechanism provides innovative financing solutions—primarily trade credit guarantees and grants—to accelerate fertilizer access and use in AfDB Regional Member Countries (RMCs) across the continent. The AFFM operates through a 2022–2028 strategic plan with three pillars: (i) strengthening the fertilizer sector through access to finance; (ii) supporting policy reforms; and (iii) facilitating smallholder farmers' access to inputs and technical assistance. Active projects have been implemented in Tanzania, Kenya, Uganda, Mozambique, Zimbabwe, Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, and Nigeria. The 2024 work program budget totals USD 18.58 million, including USD 1.20 million for new activities. This is not an open, competitive Call for Proposals; it is an institutional program page describing an ongoing AfDB financing mechanism. No submission deadline or open application process for external organizations (such as the ILO) exists. The mechanism is ineligible for ILO participation as it targets African national implementing partners, financial institutions, and agro-dealers in RMCs.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The AFFM focuses on agricultural fertilizer financing and food security in Africa, which has minimal direct alignment with ILO's core mandate on labour standards, employment, social protection, and decent work, though there is a very marginal connection through rural employment and smallholder farmer livelihoods.

results.proposal_summary: The African Green Banks Initiative (AGBI) is a pan-African initiative led by the African Development Bank, launched at COP27, in partnership with the Climate Investment Funds, Canada Climate Action, the Green Bank Network, Amundi, and the Multilateral Cooperation Center for Development Finance (MCDF). Its objective is to create an ecosystem of Green Banks across Africa by mobilising additional climate finance for green and sustainable growth aligned with countries' Nationally Determined Contributions. The initiative targets a $1.5 billion ecosystem of green investment facilities by 2030 through a blended finance approach comprising Technical Assistance and a Green Finance Facility. It is active across all African countries. This page is an informational overview — it does not constitute a Call for Proposals and provides no grant application process, eligible applicant list, or submission deadline.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The AGBI focuses on climate finance mobilisation and green banking in Africa, which has only tangential relevance to ILO's mandate on decent work and just transitions, but does not directly address labour standards, employment, or social protection.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Trade Fund (AfTra) is a multi-million dollar trade-related technical assistance facility established by the African Development Bank (AfDB) in March 2012, with seed financing from the Government of Canada. Its objective is to accelerate the integration of African countries and regional economic communities (RECs) into regional and global trading systems, complementing the Bank's operations in agriculture, infrastructure, governance, private sector development, and science and technology. The fund covers all African Development Bank member countries across the continent. No active Call for Proposals, submission deadline, per-grant funding amount, or applicant eligibility criteria are currently specified on the fund's webpage, making this an informational/program overview page rather than an open funding opportunity as of April 2026.

results.proposal_summary: The African Beneficial Ownership Transparency Network (AfBOTN) is an initiative launched in 2023 by the African Development Bank in partnership with the UK Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) and other partners. Its purpose is to bring together international partners and African governments to support regional priorities in driving reforms around beneficial ownership transparency across Africa, combating illicit financial flows, money laundering, and corruption. The network focuses on peer learning, information sharing, and practical guidance for implementing beneficial ownership registers across African countries. This page is an informational description of the network initiative and does not constitute a Call for Proposals; no grant funding, application deadline, eligible applicant categories, or grant amounts are specified.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the initiative relates to governance and anti-corruption in Africa, it does not directly address ILO core mandate areas such as labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The African Carbon Support Program is an informational program page from the African Development Bank (AfDB), describing a technical assistance initiative launched on 4 November 2010 and implemented by AfDB's Energy, Environment and Climate Change Department. The program, funded by a one-time US$1 million grant from the Fund for African Private Sector Assistance (FAPA, established by AfDB and the Japanese government), aims to assist AfDB regional member countries in accessing carbon finance under the Clean Development Mechanism (CDM). Activities include screening investment proposals for carbon finance potential, building capacity of Designated National Authorities, developing project documentation (PINs and PDDs), supporting regional grid emission factor development, and helping project owners commercialize carbon credits. This is not an open Call for Proposals; there is no submission deadline, no defined eligible applicant categories, and no open grant competition. The ILO and other external organizations cannot apply.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The program focuses on carbon finance and climate change mitigation under the CDM in Africa, which has only marginal connection to ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, and decent work, though just transition themes are tangentially relevant.

results.proposal_summary: The African Guarantee Fund (AGF) is an initiative created in 2011 by the African Development Bank, the Danish Government (DANIDA), and the Spanish Government (AECID) to facilitate access to financing for small and medium enterprises (SMEs) across Africa. The AGF provides guarantees to financial institutions — not grants — enabling them to increase their lending to SMEs as engines of growth in African economies. This AfDB web page is purely informational and does not constitute a Call for Proposals; there is no grant funding available, no application deadline, and no defined eligible applicants for a grant programme. As a guarantee instrument targeting financial institutions, the ILO would not be an eligible applicant, and the page fails multiple hard exclusion criteria including the funding instrument type and absence of a deadline.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The AGF focuses on SME finance and private sector development in Africa, which has indirect links to ILO themes of employment creation and sustainable enterprises, but the instrument is a financial guarantee product for banks, not aligned with ILO's programmatic mandate on labour standards, social protection, or decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) 2026 for Guyana and Suriname, administered by Global Affairs Canada, supports small-scale, high-impact local development projects aligned with Canada's thematic priorities including inclusive growth, democratic governance, and peace and security. Eligible recipients include local and international NGOs, intergovernmental and multilateral organizations (including the ILO, provided it works with local partners on local projects), academic institutions, Canadian NGOs, and government institutions of the recipient countries. The maximum grant per project is CAD$100,000 (average CAD$50,000), provided as a non-reimbursable contribution. Projects must be completed by February 26, 2027 (single-year) or February 25, 2028 (two-year), covering the period from April 2026. Completed applications using the designated form must be submitted by email to GRGTNG@international.gc.ca by April 30, 2026 at 23:59 GMT-4. No co-financing requirement or specific accreditation is mandated.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the CFLI covers some themes relevant to ILO's mandate (e.g., technical/vocational training and inclusive markets in Guyana), the primary focus is on peace and security, anti-trafficking, and democratic governance rather than core ILO priorities such as labour standards, social protection, or social dialogue, and the small grant size limits strategic alignment.

results.exclusion_reason: Grant size below threshold: maximum allocation is CAD 100,000 (~USD 72,000) but the average contribution is CAD 20,000–40,000, which at current exchange rates is well below USD 50,000. Additionally, the ILO Eligibility criterion is borderline: while intergovernmental organizations are listed as eligible, they must work with local partners on local projects, and the thematic priorities (governance, peace/security, women's economic rights) do not align closely with ILO's core mandate. The grant size is the primary exclusion trigger.
results.classification_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) – ASEAN 2026 is a small-grant programme administered by Global Affairs Canada, targeting small-scale, high-impact projects across ASEAN countries. Eligible recipients include local NGOs, academic institutions, international NGOs, intergovernmental organizations, and government institutions working at the local level. The maximum grant is CAD 100,000 (average CAD 20,000–40,000), which converts to approximately USD 73,000 at most (below average is well under USD 50,000). Thematic priorities are inclusive governance, peace and security, and inclusive growth/women's economic security. The deadline is April 30, 2026, which is in the future. However, the typical grant size falls well below USD 50,000, triggering the grant size exclusion criterion.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CfP lists eligible recipients including 'Intergovernmental, multilateral and regional institutions, organizations and agencies working on local development activities.' The ILO, as an intergovernmental organization, could technically fit this category provided it works with local partners on local projects. However, eligibility is conditional on working locally, which may be limiting.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The CFLI is described as a contribution/grant programme providing non-reimbursable funding to eligible recipients. The text refers to 'CFLI contribution' and 'Contribution Agreement,' confirming this is a non-repayable grant.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The maximum allocation is CAD 100,000 (approximately USD 73,000 at current rates), but the average contribution is CAD 20,000–40,000 (approximately USD 14,700–29,400), which is well below the USD 50,000 threshold. Even the maximum CAD 100,000 is only marginally above USD 50,000 and the programme is explicitly designed for small-scale projects averaging far below threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline for submission is April 30, 2026, at 23:59 (GMT+7), which is after today's date of April 22, 2026.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a grant programme for project proposals, not a procurement or consultancy tender. No RFP, ITB, or consultant language is used.

results.proposal_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) – ASEAN 2026, administered by Global Affairs Canada, provides small-scale non-reimbursable grants of CAD 20,000–100,000 to support high-impact projects across ASEAN member states (Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Vietnam). Projects must impact at least three ASEAN countries and align with thematic priorities including inclusive governance, peace and security, and growth that works for everyone (with emphasis on women's economic rights). Priority sub-themes include AI governance, Women, Peace and Security, democratic governance, youth and peacebuilding, and maritime cooperation. Eligible applicants include local NGOs, academic institutions, international NGOs, intergovernmental organizations, and government institutions working locally, as well as Canadian not-for-profit organizations. Projects must be completed by February 26, 2027. Applications must be submitted by April 30, 2026 at 23:59 GMT+7 to aseancanadacfli@gmail.com. No co-financing requirement is mandated.

results.proposal_summary: Norway (via Norad and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs) is allocating 100 million NOK for private sector development in Ukraine in 2026 and 2027, under the Nansen Support Programme for Ukraine (budget item 159.73). The call aims to promote investment-ready projects, facilitate increased private investment in Ukraine, mobilise private capital, create new jobs, and improve framework conditions for the private sector. Priority sectors include sustainable energy production, food security and agriculture, and reconstruction of critical infrastructure. The call is open to all applicants from any country (Ukrainian, international, and Norwegian), including multilateral organisations, non-profit organisations, commercial organisations, and state organisations that are legal entities with demonstrated technical and administrative competence. Applications must be submitted via the Grants Portal by 17 June 2026. Co-financing is not mandatory but can be included. Indirect costs are capped at 7% of direct operational costs. There is no explicitly stated minimum grant size; the total pool is 100 million NOK across 2026-2027.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call explicitly targets private sector development and job creation in Ukraine—which touches on ILO mandate areas of employment and enterprise development—it is primarily focused on investment mobilisation and commercial enterprise rather than labour standards, social protection, social dialogue, or core decent work elements that are central to the ILO's mandate.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The call requires applicants to be legal entities with a Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) number for Canadian organizations, implying the eligible applicant pool is limited to legal entities (NGOs, civil society, academic institutions, etc.) that can comply with Canadian contribution agreement terms. The ILO as a UN/international organization is not listed among eligible entity types and the requirement for a CRA number or equivalent legal entity status effectively excludes international organizations. Additionally, this is a rolling/unsolicited mechanism with no specific deadline, but the funding quantum and eligible applicant criteria do not accommodate UN agencies.

A repayable/innovative finance component is mentioned as an exception, but the primary instrument is a non-reimbursable contribution.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No minimum or maximum grant size is stated anywhere in the main page or guidance documents provided.

Deadline is future.evidence: This is a rolling/unsolicited mechanism with no fixed submission deadline. Applications can be submitted at any time through the Partners@International portal. No specific deadline date is mentioned.

results.proposal_summary: Global Affairs Canada (GAC) operates a rolling unsolicited concept note mechanism for international assistance grants aligned with Canada's Feminist International Assistance Policy. There is no fixed submission deadline; organizations submit concept notes at any time through the Partners@International portal. If a concept note is of interest, GAC invites the applicant to submit a full proposal. Eligible applicants are legal entities (Canadian organizations must have a CRA number); UN agencies and international organizations are not explicitly included. No grant size minimum or maximum is specified, and amounts must be quoted in Canadian dollars. The geographic scope of funded projects is global (developing countries). The two-stage process involves a concept note followed by a full proposal invitation; funding is not guaranteed until a contribution agreement is signed. The ILO, as a UN intergovernmental organization, does not clearly fit the implied eligible applicant categories.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While GAC's Feminist International Assistance Policy can encompass labour rights, gender equality at work, and social protection themes relevant to ILO's mandate, the funding mechanism is primarily designed for NGOs and legal entities rather than UN agencies, and the CfP does not specifically target ILO core themes such as labour standards, employment policy, or social dialogue.

The page does not constitute an active Call for Proposals.

results.proposal_summary: This Global Affairs Canada page provides an overview of two 5-year innovative finance pilot programs: the International Assistance Innovation Program and the Sovereign Loans Program. The programs aim to diversify Canada's international assistance toolkit and support achievement of the UN SDGs by offering flexible financing arrangements including sovereign loans, conditionally repayable contributions, and private finance mobilization tools. The page is informational and does not constitute an active Call for Proposals; no submission deadline, grant size, or eligible applicant categories are specified. The primary funding instruments described include reimbursable and conditionally repayable contributions, making this opportunity ineligible for ILO as there is no clearly defined non-reimbursable grant window with an open application process.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While Canada's international assistance programs broadly support sustainable development goals, this page describes innovative finance mechanisms (loans, repayable contributions) with no specific focus on labour standards, employment, social protection, or other ILO mandate areas, and is not an active grant call.

results.classification_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) is a Canadian government grant program funding small, high-impact projects in over 120 ODA-eligible countries. The eligible applicant list explicitly includes 'Intergovernmental, multilateral, and regional organizations', which covers the ILO as a UN/intergovernmental organization. Several country-specific calls remain open with future deadlines (earliest open deadline: 2026-04-21 for Cambodia, which has already passed, but multiple others remain open through April–May 2026). The average grant is ~$31,000 CAD, which is below the USD 50,000 threshold. However, the program description does not state a fixed maximum and individual grants could exceed USD 50,000 depending on the country call. Given ambiguity, the threshold criterion is marked unknown. The funding is non-reimbursable (grant). Overall the ILO can apply as an intergovernmental organization.

results.proposal_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI), funded by the Government of Canada with an annual budget of CAD 26.8 million, supports approximately 650 small, high-impact projects annually in over 120 ODA-eligible countries. Projects average CAD 31,000 each and focus on inclusive governance, gender equality, peace and security, economic growth, environmental action, and human dignity. Eligible applicants include local NGOs, community groups, non-profits, academic institutions, international NGOs, intergovernmental and multilateral organizations (including the ILO), regional organizations, recipient-country government agencies, and Canadian NGOs. Applications are managed by Canadian Embassies and High Commissions in each country, with country-specific calls and deadlines; multiple calls are currently open through April–May 2026. No co-financing is required. The ILO, as an intergovernmental organization, may apply in partnership with local organizations.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The CFLI covers themes relevant to ILO's mandate including fair jobs, women's economic rights, entrepreneurship, and decent work, but the program primarily targets small local civil society organizations with modest grants averaging ~CAD 31,000, which is well below ILO's typical project scale, limiting practical alignment.

results.proposal_summary: This page from Sida (Sweden's development cooperation agency) provides an informational update — not an active call for proposals — regarding the CSO Strategy 2025–2029. The update clarifies three key decisions: party-affiliated organisations (PAOs) are excluded from eligibility under this strategy; own funds (15% co-financing requirement) must consist solely of privately raised resources; and three-year agreements with extension options are permitted despite the strategy's flexibility requirements. The first call for proposals was conducted in 2024, and applicants were informed of indicative amounts and contract lengths in June 2025. A new call for major support is planned for 2027 with agreements expected from 2028. Eligible applicants are civil society organisations only; international organisations such as the ILO are not eligible. No active submission deadline exists.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While Sida's CSO strategy broadly supports civil society and development cooperation that may touch on ILO-related themes such as rights and equality, this call is exclusively for civil society organisations and does not align with ILO's mandate or eligibility as an applicant.

results.proposal_summary: This Sida webpage is a directory listing of research calls and grants supported by the Swedish International Development Cooperation Agency (Sida) and administered by various international partner organizations. The programs cover areas including antimicrobial resistance research (JPIAMR), artificial intelligence for development (AI4D Africa), women in science (OWSD), basic sciences (TWAS), early career research (IFS), economics capacity building in sub-Saharan Africa (AERC), social sciences in the Arab region (ACSS), health policy and systems research (AHPSR), clinical trials for poverty-related diseases (EDCTP), and development economics (UNU-WIDER). Eligibility is restricted to individual researchers, PhD students, early career scientists, and academic/research institutions predominantly from low-income, lower-middle-income, or science and technology-lagging countries. No unified deadline, grant size, or application process is specified on this directory page. The ILO, as an international organization, is not among the eligible applicant types for any of the listed programs.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This directory of research grants primarily targets individual researchers and academic institutions in developing countries across natural sciences, AI, economics, and health research, with no focus on labour standards, employment, social protection, social dialogue, or other core ILO mandate areas.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The CfP explicitly lists eligible applicant types as non-profit organizations, non-governmental organizations, and commercial entities. UN agencies/international organizations are not listed as eligible applicants in the main categories, though a separate UN entity self-certification form exists as an annex. However, the core selection criteria and eligibility focus on organizations with evaluation/research experience in AgTech/FinTech for rural smallholders, and the text describes 'Non-profit organizations, non-governmental organizations, and commercial entities are welcome to apply' as the eligible categories. The ILO does not clearly fit into these listed categories as an implementing partner applicant. Additionally, the co-financing requirement (minimum 20% for non-private sector, 25% for private sector) is a mandatory condition.
results.classification_summary: This IFAD Call for Proposals seeks an implementing partner for a grant to generate evidence on AgTech and FinTech solutions for rural smallholders through the Innovatech programme. Eligible applicants are non-profit organizations, NGOs, and commercial entities. The ILO, as a UN/international organization, does not clearly fit the listed eligible applicant categories. The deadline is 13 May 2026, which is in the future. Co-financing of at least 20% is mandatory. The grant is non-reimbursable. Grant size is not explicitly stated but appears above USD 50,000 based on project scope. The CfP is flagged for mandatory co-financing and has consortium leadership rules (lead must be the proponent institution, with partners sub-contracted).

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CfP states 'Non-profit organizations, non-governmental organizations, and commercial entities are welcome to apply.' ILO as a UN specialized agency/international organization does not clearly fit into these listed categories. While a UN entity self-certification form exists (Annex 2), the eligible applicant list does not include UN agencies or international organizations as a distinct eligible category for this implementing partner role.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The CfP explicitly refers to a 'grant' throughout, including 'IFAD grant resources,' 'grant financing,' and 'Grant Concept Note.' This is a non-reimbursable grant instrument.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The specific grant amount is not stated in the available documents. However, given the scope of the project (multi-country evaluation across LAC and NEN regions, multiple years, with 20% co-financing requirement), the grant is likely well above USD 50,000. Cannot confirm with certainty from available text.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline for submitting applications is 13 May 2026 Rome time (UTC +2), which is after today's date of 2026-04-22.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a Call for Proposals for a grant implementing partner, not a procurement tender for goods/services. It uses grant terminology and grant agreement frameworks throughout.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The ToR explicitly states: 'all proposals should include at least 20% of co-financing (in-cash and/or in-kind). Proposals from for-profit Private Sector entities should include at least 25% of co-financing (in-cash and/or in-kind).' Co-financing is a mandatory compliance requirement with minimum recipient contribution requirements.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required. Eligibility is based on self-certification and IFAD's own due diligence processes.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The LoI states: 'only the proponent institution shall be responsible for achieving the project objectives and outcomes...while the partner institution(s) will be sub-contracted by the proponent.' The lead/proponent institution does not need to be a non-UN entity per se, but leadership rules are present requiring a single lead proponent.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: No restriction to applicants from any specific country. The call is open internationally to non-profit organizations, NGOs, and commercial entities without geographic restriction on applicant registration.

results.proposal_summary: IFAD is seeking an implementing partner for a grant titled 'Evidence for Scale-Up of AgTech and FinTech Solutions through IFAD's PoLG' (Reference No. 2000005624), published on 21 April 2026. The grant aims to generate robust evidence and lessons learned to support the expansion of financial and non-financial services for rural smallholders delivered by AgTech and FinTech companies under IFAD's Innovatech programme, including ex-post evaluation in Latin America and the Caribbean (Innovatech 1.0 and 2.0) and ex-ante assessment in Near East, North Africa, Europe, and Central Asia (Jordan, Uzbekistan, Turkey). The implementing partner will also develop an investor-oriented impact reporting methodology. Eligible applicants are non-profit organizations, NGOs, and commercial entities; UN agencies/international organizations are not listed as eligible. All proposals must include at least 20% co-financing (25% for private sector), and grant budget amounts are not specified. The deadline for submission is 13 May 2026 (Rome time, UTC+2). A Q&A session is scheduled for 4 May 2026 (per LoI) or 6 May 2026 (per ToR). Proposals must be submitted in English to lacgrants@ifad.org.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This CfP focuses on AgTech/FinTech solutions, rural smallholder agriculture, and impact evaluation in the context of rural development, which has limited alignment with the ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment policy, social protection, and decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The Australian Government Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (DFAT) has issued an Expression of Interest (DFAT 1091) on AusTender for procurement of management advisory services to implement the Primary Health Care for Papua New Guinea (PHC4PNG) program. The program aims to support PNG in delivering its National Health Plan 2021-2030 by strengthening primary health care systems, governance, and services, particularly in rural and remote areas. It covers communicable diseases, maternal and child health, and non-communicable diseases, with a health innovation fund component. The implementation period is December 2026 to December 2031 with a 5-year extension option. This is a procurement tender (service contract), not a grant, with the EOI closing on 5 May 2026. No estimated contract value is stated. The ILO is not eligible as this is a procurement exercise for management advisory services.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This procurement call focuses on primary health care delivery in PNG and does not align with ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, social protection policy, or decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The Italian Agency for International Cooperation and Development (AICS), through its Tirana office, is launching a grant call for the initiative 'SHAR DINARIC - Sustainable Development and Protection of Natural and Cultural Heritage in the Transborder Dinaric Alps' (AID 013071/01/0). The initiative is divided into two lots: Lot 1 (€1,500,000) for sustainable tourism development in the Dinaric Alps transborder area in North Macedonia, and Lot 2 (€1,500,000) for the same in Kosovo, for a total budget of €3,000,000. Eligible applicants are exclusively Civil Society Organizations (OSC) and non-profit entities registered on the Italian list under Article 26(3) of Law 125/2014, either as single applicants or in temporary consortia (ATS). The initiative aims to develop sustainable tourism, protect natural and cultural heritage, and improve socioeconomic conditions of local communities in the Shar Mountain National Parks area. The deadline for submission of executive proposals is 1 July 2026 at 13:00. Co-financing is not mandatory. The ILO is not eligible as it does not meet the strict applicant eligibility requirements.

results.proposal_summary: AICS (Italian Agency for Development Cooperation), Amman Office, has launched a Call for Proposals (AID 013390/01/0) for an integrated emergency programme focused on child protection and support to vulnerable families in Jordan. The total budget is EUR 3,500,000, with EUR 3,350,000 allocated to the child protection sector and EUR 150,000 for management costs. The programme targets refugees (primarily Syrian) and host communities in Jordan, with a focus on children at risk of exploitation, gender-based violence victims, persons with disabilities, and female-headed households. Eligible applicants are strictly non-profit organizations (OSC) - either Italian entities registered in the AICS list per Law 125/2014, or foreign non-profits without Italian operational headquarters meeting specific criteria. The submission deadline is 1 June 2026 at 12:00. The ILO, as an international/intergovernmental organization, is not eligible under the defined applicant categories.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the programme addresses humanitarian protection, child welfare, and social support in Jordan, it does not directly align with core ILO mandate areas such as labour standards, employment policy, social dialogue, or decent work - though it tangentially touches on child labour prevention and social protection themes.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The guidelines define a strict and exhaustive list of eligible lead applicants (NGOs, private companies, public sector operators, local authorities, or inter-governmental organizations as defined by Article 159(1) of the EU Financial Regulation). While ILO as an inter-governmental organization could technically fall under Article 159(1), the guidelines also require the lead applicant to be 'registered towards the relevant authority in Uganda i.e. legitimated to fully operate in Uganda' and 'effectively established in a Member State of the European Union or in an eligible country or territory as defined under Article 28(1) of NDICI-GE' — though the latter obligation does not apply to international organisations. However, more critically, the guidelines require the lead applicant to demonstrate at least 7 years of experience in incubation and acceleration of MSMEs preferably in NTFPs Value Chains and/or Rural Development, and the capacity to manage a yearly project portfolio of at least EUR 1,000,000. These are specialized sectoral requirements (non-timber forest products value chains, MSME incubation) outside the ILO's core mandate. Additionally, the CfP is focused on forest conservation and non-timber value chain enterprise development — a domain not squarely within ILO's mandate. The primary exclusion reason is that while ILO could be considered an inter-governmental organization eligible in principle, the operational eligibility requirements (NTFP/MSME incubation expertise, Uganda registration) make ILO an implausible fit, and the ILO match score is very low.
results.classification_summary: This is a restricted Call for Proposals by AICS (Italian Agency for Development Cooperation) Kampala office, funded by the EU under the ReForEst Uganda project (NDICI AFRICA/2023/450-049). It seeks one grantee to implement Output 3 of the ReForEst Uganda project: developing sustainable non-timber forest product (NTFP) value chain enterprises in Eastern Uganda and Napak District, Karamoja. The grant range is EUR 3,000,000 to EUR 4,000,000. Eligible lead applicants must be NGOs, private companies, public sector operators, local authorities, or inter-governmental organizations registered in Uganda with at least 7 years of MSME incubation/acceleration experience. The deadline for concept note submission is 7 May 2026. While ILO could qualify as an inter-governmental organization under Article 159(1) of the EU Financial Regulation, the highly specialized eligibility requirements (NTFP/MSME incubation experience, Uganda registration) and the thematic focus (forest conservation, non-timber value chains) make this opportunity a poor fit for ILO. The ILO match score is very low.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The guidelines (art. 2.1.1) define eligible lead applicants as NGOs, private companies, public sector operators, local authorities, or international (inter-governmental) organizations as defined by Article 159(1) of the EU Financial Regulation — registered in Uganda and legitimated to fully operate there. While ILO is an inter-governmental organization, the guidelines also require a minimum of 7 years' experience in incubation and acceleration of MSMEs preferably in NTFPs Value Chains and/or Rural Development, and demonstration of capacity to manage a yearly project portfolio of at least EUR 1,000,000. These specialized sectoral requirements are outside ILO's mandate and expertise, effectively excluding it from eligibility as a lead applicant. ILO is not explicitly listed as an eligible entity type in the context of this specialized call.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The document explicitly states this is a Call for Proposals for a non-reimbursable grant, with amounts between EUR 3,000,000 and EUR 4,000,000.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The minimum grant amount is EUR 3,000,000 and maximum is EUR 4,000,000, both well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline for submission of concept notes is May 7th, 2026 at 16:00 EAT, which is in the future relative to today's date of April 22, 2026.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a Call for Proposals for a grant, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The guidelines state the grant may cover up to 100% of eligible costs. Co-financing is not mandatory; the guidelines note that if full financing is requested, the applicant must justify it, but it is not excluded.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body (e.g., GEF, Green Climate Fund) is required. Note that lead applicants whose pillars have been positively assessed by the European Commission may sign a contribution agreement instead of a standard grant contract, but this is not a prerequisite for applying.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The guidelines do not specify that consortium leadership must be held by a non-UN entity. The lead applicant categories include inter-governmental organizations.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Lead applicants must be effectively established in a Member State of the European Union or in an eligible country or territory as defined under Article 28(1) of NDICI-GE (though this does not apply to international organizations), and must be registered/legitimated to fully operate in Uganda.

results.proposal_summary: The Italian Agency for Development Cooperation (AICS), acting as contracting authority under the EU-funded ReForEst Uganda project (NDICI AFRICA/2023/450-049, total budget EUR 14 million), is issuing a restricted Call for Proposals (ReForEst/CFP/2026/03) to identify one grantee to implement Output 3 of the project: developing sustainable non-timber forest product (NTFP) value chain enterprises and strategies for enhanced livelihoods in Uganda's Eastern Region and Napak District (Karamoja). The grant amount ranges from EUR 3,000,000 to EUR 4,000,000, covering up to 100% of eligible costs. Eligible lead applicants must be NGOs, private companies, public sector operators, local authorities, or inter-governmental organizations, registered and legitimated to operate in Uganda, and effectively established in an EU Member State or NDICI-GE eligible country (exception applies to international organizations). Lead applicants must have at least 7 years of MSME incubation/acceleration experience in NTFP value chains and/or rural development. The call follows a two-stage process: concept notes are due by 7 May 2026 at 16:00 EAT, with up to 5 pre-selected applicants later invited to submit full proposals. The action targets 16 districts across Eastern Uganda and must promote 5 NTFP value chains (cashew, wild mango, honey, sheanut, bamboo), incubate 140 enterprises, support 50 production groups, and sustain 5,000 jobs.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This CfP is primarily focused on forest conservation, non-timber forest product value chain development, and MSME incubation in Uganda — areas peripheral to the ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment policy, social protection, and social dialogue, with only minor overlap in job creation and rural livelihoods.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The CfP explicitly restricts eligible applicants to non-profit entities (soggetti non profit / OSC - Organizzazioni della Società Civile) registered in Italy or in the partner country (Sudan). International Organizations such as the ILO are not listed as eligible applicants. The FAQ further clarifies that lead applicants must be OSCs registered in the AICS list pursuant to Article 26 of Law 125/2014, which covers civil society organizations, not UN agencies.

Eligible applicants are strictly limited to non-profit organizations (OSC) registered in Italy or in Sudan. The ILO, as a UN/international organization, is not eligible. The deadline for submission is 4 May 2026.

The FAQ confirms lead applicants must be OSCs registered in this list. The ILO as an international/intergovernmental organization is not included in these eligible categories.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The CfP provides non-reimbursable contributions (grants) to selected non-profit organizations for emergency project implementation in Sudan. The document refers to 'finanziamento dei progetti di emergenza' (financing of emergency projects) as grants.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Total available funding is €4,500,000 for Lot 1. The FAQ mentions a maximum contribution of EUR 1,200,000 per project, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline for submission is 04-05-2026 (4 May 2026) at 17:00, which is in the future relative to today's date of 2026-04-22.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a Call for Proposals (CfP) for project grants, not a procurement tender or consultancy contract.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No mandatory co-financing requirement is mentioned in the available document text.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: Applicants must be registered in the AICS list of OSCs pursuant to Article 26, paragraph 3, of Law 125/2014 (Italian law on development cooperation). This constitutes a specific pre-existing accreditation/registration requirement.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The FAQ specifies that if a local organization (developing country-based NGO) capable of demonstrating operational capacity is part of an ATS (consortium), it must assume the role of lead entity (Lead Partner), under penalty of exclusion. This restricts consortium leadership to non-UN entities.

This restricts eligibility to Italy-based or Sudan-based organizations.

results.proposal_summary: The Italian Agency for Development Cooperation (AICS), through its Niamey office, has launched a Call for Proposals for the emergency initiative 'ReSSARC: Rafforzamento del Settore Sanitario e della Sicurezza Alimentare nella Repubblica Centrafricana' (AID 013381/01/0). With a total budget of €1,800,000 (plus €200,000 in management costs), the initiative aims to strengthen health systems, combat malnutrition, and improve food security for vulnerable populations in the Central African Republic, with a focus on maternal and child health and gender-sensitive approaches. Only non-profit organizations (Italian OSCs/NGOs), either registered on the AICS list or meeting specific eligibility criteria, are eligible to apply, as single applicants (up to €800,000) or in consortium/ATS (up to €1,000,000). The deadline for submission is 5 May 2026 at 12:00 (Niamey time) via certified email (PEC). No mandatory co-financing is required.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This CfP focuses on emergency humanitarian health and food security interventions in the Central African Republic, with limited alignment to ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection systems, or decent work; only tangential links through social protection and vulnerable populations.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The CfP explicitly restricts eligible applicants to non-profit entities ('soggetti non profit') registered in Italy (OSC/NGOs inscribed in the AICS list per art. 26) and locally-registered non-profit organisations in Ethiopia. The ILO as an international/intergovernmental organisation does not fall within these categories.
results.classification_summary: This is a Call for Proposals by AICS (Italian Agency for Development Cooperation) for humanitarian response strengthening in Ethiopia, with a total budget of EUR 10.4 million across three components. The call is restricted to Italian-registered non-profit organisations (OSC) inscribed in the AICS list and their partners. The deadline has been extended to 27 April 2026 (still future as of today). ILO is not eligible as it is an international organisation, not a non-profit entity registered in Italy.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CfP explicitly states it selects projects 'presentati da soggetti non profit' (submitted by non-profit entities) per art. 19 of AICS statute. Section 5 (Requisiti di partecipazione) and the FAQ make clear that eligible lead applicants must be OSC (Civil Society Organisations) inscribed in the AICS official list per art. 26, which is a registry for Italian non-profit organisations. The ILO as a UN/international intergovernmental organisation does not fit this category.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The CfP provides non-reimbursable grants ('contributi') to selected non-profit organisations for humanitarian projects. This is a standard grant instrument with no repayment obligation.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The three lots total EUR 10.4 million (Lot 1: EUR 5,200,000; Lot 2: EUR 1,500,000; Lot 3: EUR 3,700,000). Individual grants can be up to EUR 700,000 per single organisation or up to EUR 1,500,000 for joint proposals, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline was extended to 27 April 2026 at 17:00 (Addis Abeba time), as confirmed by the extension notice document dated 20/03/2026. Today is 22 April 2026, so the deadline is still in the future.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a Call for Proposals (CfP) for project grants, not a procurement or consultancy tender. No RFP/ITB language is used.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No mandatory co-financing requirement is mentioned in the available CfP text or FAQ documents.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: Lead applicants must be inscribed in the official AICS list ('elenco') per art. 26, which constitutes a specific pre-existing accreditation/registration requirement with AICS.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The CfP specifies that in an ATS (Temporary Association), the lead entity (mandataria/capofila) must be an OSC inscribed in the AICS list. Developing country-based NGOs that demonstrate local operational capacity may be required to assume the lead role. These are consortium leadership rules specific to non-UN entities.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The CfP requires lead applicants (proponenti/mandatari) to be Italian non-profit organisations inscribed in the AICS list per art. 26, which implies Italian registration/legal establishment.

results.proposal_summary: The Italian Agency for Development Cooperation (AICS), through its Addis Abeba office, has issued a Call for Proposals (AID 013303/01/0) for a EUR 10.4 million humanitarian response initiative in Ethiopia titled 'Rafforzamento della risposta umanitaria in Etiopia.' The initiative is structured across three components: Component 1 (EUR 5.2M) addresses conflict-related needs in northern Ethiopia (Tigray, Afar, Amhara); Component 2 (EUR 1.5M) responds to the complex South Sudanese and Sudanese crisis and displacement in Gambella and Benishangul Gumuz; and Component 3 (EUR 3.7M) addresses extreme climate phenomena in Oromia, Somali, South West, South Ethiopia, Central, and Sidama regions. Eligible applicants are Italian non-profit civil society organisations (OSC) inscribed in the AICS official list per art. 26, with individual grants capped at EUR 700,000 per single organisation and EUR 1,500,000 for joint ATS consortia. No co-financing requirement is specified. The submission deadline has been extended to 27 April 2026 at 17:00 (Addis Abeba time). The ILO is not eligible as it is an international organisation, not an Italian-registered non-profit entity.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While this CfP addresses humanitarian response in Ethiopia, which has tangential links to ILO's work on decent work in crisis situations, it is primarily focused on food security, health, nutrition, WASH and protection activities rather than core ILO mandate areas such as labour standards, employment, social protection or social dialogue.

While International Organizations can serve as Partners, the ILO cannot be the Soggetto Proponente (lead applicant).

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The bando requires the Soggetto Proponente (lead) to be an OSC inscribed in the AICS list (Italian CSO), effectively requiring non-UN/non-ILO entities to lead any consortium. International Organizations can only participate as Partners.

This call is funded under NDICI AFRICA/2023/441-988. ILO as an international organisation is explicitly included.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call is explicitly titled 'Call for proposals' with grant awards (Lot 1: €4,000,000 and Lot 2: €800,000). The document references 'grant contract' and 'grant applicants', confirming non-reimbursable grant funding.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Lot 1 is €4,000,000 and Lot 2 is €800,000, both well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline for submission is 22 May 2026 at 17:00 GMT+3, which is after today's date of 2026-04-22.

results.proposal_summary: The Italian Agency for Development Cooperation (AICS), acting under the EU NDICI AFRICA/2023/441-988 programme, has launched a delegated cooperation Call for Proposals (Grant/02/HealthPro II) for primary healthcare provision to vulnerable populations affected by food insecurity and malnutrition in Sudan. The call has two lots: Lot 1 (€4,000,000) and Lot 2 (€800,000), targeting interventions in Red Sea, Gedaref, White Nile, and Khartoum states in Sudan. The submission deadline is 22 May 2026 at 17:00 GMT+3. International organisations (including UN agencies such as ILO) and legal entities from NDICI-eligible countries are eligible to apply as lead or co-applicants. The call follows a two-step evaluation: concept notes are assessed first, followed by full application evaluation for pre-selected applicants. The programme responds to Sudan's severe humanitarian crisis, including near-total health system collapse, widespread famine, and disease outbreaks following the April 2023 conflict.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This call focuses on primary healthcare provision and nutrition in a humanitarian crisis in Sudan, which is outside ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection policy, and social dialogue, though minor tangential links exist through crisis-affected decent work conditions.

results.proposal_summary: The TriNova 2026 Call for Triangular Cooperation Projects is a joint initiative by APC Colombia and Germany (BMZ/GIZ) that funds triangular cooperation projects of up to EUR 300,000 per project for a maximum duration of two years. The call is open to projects addressing any thematic area aligned with the 2030 Agenda and national development plans, involving at least one partner from Latin America, the Caribbean, or the Global South. Eligible applicants include public institutions, private sector entities, civil society organizations, academia, and NGOs — international organizations such as the ILO are not eligible. Projects must involve a requesting partner, a main partner, and TriNova as facilitating partner, with mandatory co-financing contributions (in kind or financial) from all partners. The deadline for submission is 08 May 2026, with proposals to be sent officially by email to both APC Colombia and GIZ.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call covers broad development themes with no sectoral restrictions — potentially touching on labour, skills, and decent work — ILO is explicitly ineligible as an international organization under the exhaustive applicant list, and the primary focus is on triangular cooperation modality rather than core ILO mandate areas.

results.proposal_summary: The Agence Française de Développement (AFD), on behalf of the French Government, operates several grant financing mechanisms for civil society organizations (CSOs) and NGOs working in international solidarity and development. The main instruments include the CSO Initiatives Facility (for projects ≥ €500,000 in all OECD DAC ODA-eligible countries, co-financing up to 80–90% of project costs), the now-suspended FISONG innovation facility (open to international NGOs of any nationality, funding €700k–€1M per project), the Crisis and Post-Crisis Calls for Projects (APCC, funded via Minka Fund and EU trust funds), and the Support Fund for Feminist Organizations (FSOF). Eligible applicants are strictly CSOs/NGOs — French associations, syndicates, foundations, and local civil society organizations — explicitly excluding international organizations such as the ILO. All specific calls documented on this page have closed deadlines (2018–2022), and the FISONG has been suspended since 2023. The CSO Initiatives Facility reopens annually mid-March to mid-May but no confirmed 2026 active call is documented. Mandatory co-financing of at least 10% is required for most instruments.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While some past FISONG themes (social protection, financial inclusion of migrants, informal economy) align with ILO's mandate, the ILO is explicitly ineligible as an international institution under all AFD CSO financing mechanisms, making this opportunity irrelevant for ILO regardless of thematic alignment.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not eligible: the CfP explicitly restricts applicants to accredited National Implementing Entities (NIEs) of the Adaptation Fund. ILO, as an international/UN organization, does not fall within this category.
results.classification_summary: This is the Adaptation Fund NIE Small Grants for Innovation programme, offering grants of up to USD 250,000 to accredited National Implementing Entities (NIEs) for innovative climate adaptation projects. Eligibility is strictly limited to entities with 'Accredited' NIE status with the Adaptation Fund. ILO, as an international/UN organization, does not qualify as an NIE and is therefore ineligible. The deadline is rolling (twice per year), with the next submission date listed on the AF website calendar. The programme does not directly relate to ILO's core mandate on labour standards and decent work.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The eligibility section explicitly states: 'NIEs applying for innovation grants must be a National Implementing Entity (NIE) to the Adaptation Fund and have an accreditation status of Accredited.' ILO is an international/UN organization and is not an accredited NIE of the Adaptation Fund. The list is exhaustive and ILO does not fit.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The programme provides non-reimbursable grants of up to USD 250,000 to eligible NIEs for climate adaptation innovation projects.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Grants of up to USD 250,000 are available, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The page states proposals are accepted twice a year and the next submission deadline can be found on the AF calendar and Apply for Funding page, but no specific date is provided in the text. The deadline cannot be confirmed as past or future from the available information.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a grant call for proposals, not a procurement tender or consultancy RFP.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The instructions explicitly state: 'The Adaptation Fund does not require co-financing for the projects/programmes it funds.' Co-financing is mentioned as possible but not required.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: Applicants must be an accredited National Implementing Entity (NIE) with 'Accredited' status with the Adaptation Fund. This is a mandatory pre-existing accreditation requirement.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are mentioned in the CfP text or supporting documents.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Only eligible Parties to the Kyoto Protocol or the Paris Agreement (developing country parties) can submit proposals, and applicants must be accredited NIEs from those countries, effectively restricting eligibility to entities from specific developing nations.

results.proposal_summary: The Adaptation Fund NIE Small Grants for Innovation programme invites eligible Parties to the Kyoto Protocol or the Paris Agreement to submit proposals for small-grant climate adaptation innovation projects, exclusively through accredited National Implementing Entities (NIEs). Grants of up to USD 250,000 are available, with a total set-aside of USD 2 million launched in December 2018, supporting at least 28 small grants. Projects must address innovative adaptation challenges in thematic areas including gender equality, disaster risk reduction, food security, urban adaptation, water management, and others. The programme is open to developing country NIEs with current 'Accredited' status with the Adaptation Fund. Proposals are accepted twice per year and reviewed at regular Board meetings; the next deadline is listed on the Adaptation Fund website calendar. Co-financing is not required. Applications must be submitted with a signed Letter of Endorsement from the country's Designated Authority to afbsec@adaptation-fund.org.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This CfP focuses on climate adaptation innovation (disaster risk reduction, food security, urban/water management) with no direct linkage to ILO's core mandate on labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The call explicitly restricts applicants to accredited National Implementing Entities (NIEs) of the Adaptation Fund. The ILO, as a multilateral/international organization, does not qualify as a National Implementing Entity and is not listed among eligible applicant types.
results.classification_summary: This call from the Adaptation Fund is for NIE Small Grants for Innovation and Learning, a bundled funding window combining small innovation grants (up to USD 250,000) and learning grants (up to USD 500,000) for a maximum of USD 750,000. Eligibility is strictly limited to accredited National Implementing Entities (NIEs) of the Adaptation Fund. The ILO, as an international/multilateral organization, does not qualify as an NIE and is therefore ineligible. Additionally, applicants must hold a specific pre-existing accreditation with the Adaptation Fund.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The eligibility criteria explicitly state: '(i) Must be an accredited national implementing entity of the Adaptation Fund and have an accreditation status of Accredited.' The ILO is a multilateral/international organization, not a National Implementing Entity (NIE). Multilateral Implementing Entities (MIEs) are a separate category not covered by this funding window.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The page describes non-reimbursable grant funding: 'small innovation grants' (up to USD 250,000) and 'learning grants' (up to USD 500,000), bundled up to USD 750,000. No repayment obligation is mentioned.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The bundled proposal maximum is USD 750,000, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: No fixed deadline date is stated. The page indicates proposals are accepted on a rolling basis: 'The Adaptation Fund Board accepts and considers project proposals for Small Grants on a rolling basis.' Applicants are directed to check the Apply for Funding page and calendar for the next submission deadline. No past deadline is specified.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a grant funding call, not a procurement or consultancy tender. No RFP, ITB, or consultant services language is used.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The application instructions explicitly state: 'The Adaptation Fund does not require co-financing for the projects/programmes it funds.' Co-financing is mentioned as possible but not required.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: Applicants 'Must be an accredited national implementing entity of the Adaptation Fund and have an accreditation status of Accredited.' This is a mandatory pre-existing accreditation requirement with the Adaptation Fund.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium or partnership leadership rules are mentioned in the call. Applications are made by individual NIEs.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: There is no restriction to applicants from a specific donor's domestic territory. The call is open to accredited NIEs from all eligible developing countries that are Parties to the Paris Agreement.

results.proposal_summary: The Adaptation Fund's NIE Small Grants for Innovation and Learning is a bundled funding window that allows accredited National Implementing Entities (NIEs) in eligible developing countries to combine small innovation grants (up to USD 250,000) and learning grants (up to USD 500,000) into a single submission of up to USD 750,000. The program supports concrete climate change adaptation projects that generate and capture learning, aligning with National Adaptation Plans and Nationally Determined Contributions. Eligible applicants are exclusively NIEs that hold active accreditation status with the Adaptation Fund and submit a Designated Authority endorsement letter. Applications are accepted on a rolling basis and reviewed at regular Adaptation Fund Board meetings; the next deadline is published on the Fund's website. Co-financing is not required. The ILO is ineligible as this window is restricted to National Implementing Entities.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the Adaptation Fund addresses climate adaptation in developing countries—tangentially linked to ILO's just transition and decent work agenda—this specific grant window is strictly for climate change adaptation innovation and learning by national implementing entities, with no direct focus on labour standards, employment, social protection, or other core ILO mandate areas.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not an eligible applicant. The AFCIA program targets non-profit Civil Society Organizations (CSOs), NGOs, Business Member Organizations, community groups, governments, and young innovators registered in developing countries (UNDP window), or developing country governments/entities endorsed by national focal points (UNEP-CTCN window). ILO as an international/UN organization is not listed as an eligible entity. Additionally, no active open deadline is identified; the program calls referenced (2020-2022) have all passed.
results.classification_summary: The AFCIA is a USD 10 million small grant aggregator program implemented by UNDP and UNEP/CTCN, offering competitive grants of up to USD 250,000 for innovation in climate change adaptation in developing countries. Eligible applicants under the UNDP window are not-for-profit CSOs, NGOs, BMOs, and community-based organizations registered in developing countries. The ILO, as an international organization, does not fall within the listed eligible applicant categories. Furthermore, all identified call deadlines (2020, 2021, 2022) are in the past.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The brochure explicitly states that the UNDP window is open to 'Not-for-profit, Civil Society Organisations (CSOs) including Non-Governmental Organisations (NGOs), Business Member Associations (BMOs), and other Associations, Cooperatives, or Community Based Organisations registered in a developing country.' The UNEP-CTCN window targets developing country governments/entities endorsed by national focal points. ILO as an international/UN agency does not fit any of these listed categories.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The program explicitly provides 'small grants' and 'micro grants' on a non-reimbursable competitive basis, up to USD 250,000 each.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Grants are up to USD 250,000 each, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The brochure indicates calls for proposals in November 2020, February 2021, and February 2022. All identified deadlines are in the past (before today, 2026-04-23). No future deadline is mentioned on the page or in attached documents.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The opportunity is a competitive grant program, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No co-financing or cost-sharing requirement is mentioned as mandatory for applicants.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: Applicants do not need prior accreditation with the Adaptation Fund. The accreditation requirement applies to implementing entities (UNDP, UNEP), not to grant recipients.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No specific consortium leadership rules requiring non-UN entity leadership are mentioned.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: For the UNDP window, applicants must be registered in a developing country. For UNEP-CTCN, eligible countries are developing countries in Africa, Asia-Pacific and Latin America and Caribbean under the UNFCCC without NIEs accredited to the Adaptation Fund.
